coredata.dc:title: Sulfication-induced non-radiative electron-hole recombination dynamics in graphene quantum dots for tuning photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Xue, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: GQDs, or graphene quantum dots, are promising materials for energy-related applications. Their optoelectronic properties can be modified by adding heteroatoms, making them good candidates for photocatalysts. However, the structure–property relationship of these materials still needs to be investigated to control their properties better. In particular, photocatalysis of GQDs is hindered by non-radiative electron-hole recombination. In this study, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to investigate the electronic structures and optical properties of GQDs doped with three distinct sulfur functional groups, i.e., sulfur oxide (O3HS), sulfhydryl (SH), and thiophene (C4H4S), respectively. The results suggest that sulfur doping decreases the GQD bandgap. In particular, the asymmetric capping of the GQD edges with the C4H4S groups led to additional peaks at low excitation energies, whereas for GQDs functionalized with O3HS or SH groups, only a shift in the main absorption peak or a change in the absorption intensity was observed. SH functionalization drastically increased electronic coupling, while C4H4S functionalization induced more charge-relaxation channels in the GQDs. Thus, the results shed light on the mechanisms governing the photocatalytic efficiency of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Density functional theory
Graphene quantum dot
Non-radiative decay
S functionalization

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of charge dynamics in a semiconductors/GQDs photoelectrochemical cell. The rapid non-radiative decay reduces the charge carriers injected into the CB of the bulk semiconductor and, thus, the charge carrier density involved in a photocatalytic reaction.
Equilibrium CC bond angle (a) before and (b) after S functionalization of GQDs.

(a)–(l) Geometric structures of GQDs functionalized with S-functional groups (sulfur oxide (O3HS), sulfhydryl (SH), and thiophene (C4H4S)) with different edge coverages and structural symmetries; (m) pristine GQD.
(a)–(l) Projected density of states (PDOS) of GQDs attached with various S-functional groups with different edge coverages.
(a)–(l) Absorption spectra of GQDs attached with various S-functional groups with different edge coverages.
(a)–(c) Calculated Huang–Rhys factors for GQDs attached with various S functionalities.
FMOs and the Kohn-sham energy levels (a.u.) of pristine GQD and SGQDs.
Calculated non-radiative rate constants and related parameters.

head.ce:title: Sulfication-induced non-radiative electron-hole recombination dynamics in graphene quantum dots for tuning photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou 362000, Fujian Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou 362000, Fujian Province, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: GQDs, or graphene quantum dots, are promising materials for energy-related applications. Their optoelectronic properties can be modified by adding heteroatoms, making them good candidates for photocatalysts. However, the structure–property relationship of these materials still needs to be investigated to control their properties better. In particular, photocatalysis of GQDs is hindered by non-radiative electron-hole recombination. In this study, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to investigate the electronic structures and optical properties of GQDs doped with three distinct sulfur functional groups, i.e., sulfur oxide (O3HS), sulfhydryl (SH), and thiophene (C4H4S), respectively. The results suggest that sulfur doping decreases the GQD bandgap. In particular, the asymmetric capping of the GQD edges with the C4H4S groups led to additional peaks at low excitation energies, whereas for GQDs functionalized with O3HS or SH groups, only a shift in the main absorption peak or a change in the absorption intensity was observed. SH functionalization drastically increased electronic coupling, while C4H4S functionalization induced more charge-relaxation channels in the GQDs. Thus, the results shed light on the mechanisms governing the photocatalytic efficiency of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, much research has been done on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are new members of the carbon family with outstanding chemical and physical properties. GQDs are particularly attractive for biological applications because their carbon skeleton is nontoxic. In addition, GQDs can easily form a heterogeneous junction with another wide-gap semiconductor for photovoltaic and photocatalytic applications. Moreover, chemical functionalization and heteroatom doping can easily change the bandgap of the GQDs, which makes them particularly attractive for solar energy applications. Doping is a very accessible method to modify the optoelectronic properties of GQDs. However, the different doping configurations significantly complicate the experimentally determined optical properties. For example, a previous study shows that increasing the pyrrole N content in GQDs promotes non-radiative decay [1], but another study reports that increasing the pyrrole N content in GQDs promotes radiative decay [2]. This contradictory observation is due to the fact that mixed N-functionalities in GQDs produce different charge relaxation channels leading to different non-radiative decay processes in GQDs. Studying the different charge relaxation channels is challenging because the synthetic route cannot prepare GQDs with the specific doping configuration. Therefore, a theoretical analysis of the effects of different doping configurations on the non-radiative relaxation dynamics would be of great benefit.

Sulfur doping of carbon materials is often used in photocatalysis. Doping the carbon skeleton with sulfur atoms changes the electronic structures of sulfur-doped carbon-based materials, although the variation of catalytic properties resulting from sulfur doping is still unknown. The non-radiative decay of excited states plays an essential role in modulating the photocatalytic efficiency of GQDs. Rapid non-radiative electron-hole recombination reduces the charge carriers at the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of GQDs, decreasing photocatalytic efficiency. Since electron-hole recombination is so important for the quantum yield of charge carriers in photocatalytic reactions such as hydrogen evolution, it is highly desirable to learn more about their dynamics (Fig. 1 ). For example, Cui et al. have investigated the electron-hole recombination rates in GQDs functionalized with epoxide and hydroxyl groups and showed that adding epoxide and hydroxyl groups to the GQD edges lowers the non-radiative decay rates in GQDs [3,4], resulting in a faster photocatalytic process. However, it has not yet been shown how sulfur doping affects the photophysical properties and thus leads to different photocatalytic efficiencies of the GQDs.

The solution of the time-dependent Schrödinger equation and the propagation of the nuclear dynamics form the basis for the ab initio non-adiabatic molecular dynamics [5,6]. This approach can best study the kinetics of charge transport, electron-hole recombination, and energy transfer in nanomaterials. Tully's fewest switch surface hopping (FSSH) [7] is a pioneering example of this type of research. A potential surface is stochastically selected for nuclear dynamics to control the nuclear trajectory. For modeling the non-radiative decay, the connection between electrons and nuclear dynamics was established by a non-adiabatic vector [8,9]. The dynamics of electron and hole relaxation in the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of GQDs were studied by Cui et al. [8] using FSSH simulations. They also investigated the dynamics of electron and hole transfer in graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) and found that the intramolecular dynamics of electron and hole transfer in GOQDs is driven by different charge relaxation channels [9]. However, simulating large molecular systems with hundreds of atoms is impractical due to the time and effort required to calculate the time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) energy gradient.

Fermi's golden rule, on the other hand, is based on the time-dependent perturbation theory. The best-known example of this method is the Marcus theory [10,11]. However, the Marcus equation is only suitable for simulating the non-radiative decay process governed by the low-frequency vibrational modes of the nucleus since a high-temperature limit is required. On the other hand, Marcus–Jortner–Levich (M−J−L) equations [12,13] are optimal for determining the non-radiative transition rates when high-frequency vibrational modes are relevant, since density functional theory (DFT) and TDDFT calculations [3,4] are well suited for determining the relevant parameters in the M−J−L equation, making them applicable for modeling the nonradiative decay rates of molecular systems in condensed phases.

In the current work, various sulfur functionalities (i.e., sulfur oxide, sulfhydryl, and thiophene) were introduced into S-doped GQDs (SGQDs) to study their effects on the electronic structure and optical properties. We have also investigated the dynamics of the non-radiative electron-hole recombination of the SGQDs in terms of electron-vibrational coupling and electron-hole interactions. The calculations provide specific details about the mechanism underlying the non-radiative decay process mediated by the S-functionalities of the GQDs.

S-functional groups (sulfur oxide (O3HS), sulfhydryl (SH), and thiophene (C4H4S)) were attached to the GQD edges at different positions and passivation densities to generate the original geometries of the SGQDs (Fig. 1). Experiments have shown that these S-functional groups are present at the GQD edges [14,15]. GQDs with one, two, or three S-functional groups and GQDs with S-functional groups doped at each sterically accessible site have been studied. For investigating the effect of doping on the electronic and optical properties of the SGQDs, a pristine GQD (C54H18) of comparable size to the SGQDs was constructed for comparison. The S-functional groups occupied two different edge positions (A1 and A2) on the GQDs. Since A1 is located at the corner of the GQD and A2 along the edge, A1 is the most thermodynamically favorable of the two positions (Supplemental Table S1), as A1 is more accessible to reach from the steric point of view than A2. The S-functional group is placed on the A1 position of the SGQD for single functionalization. The incorporation of S-functional groups at two or three A1 positions resulted in difunctionalization and trifunctionalization of the GQDs. In the latter, the S-functional groups are uniformly distributed at an angle of 120° along the GQD edges.

According to Fermi's golden rule, the non-radiative decay rate is given by [16]:

is the atomic displacement;

is the Huang–Rhys factor;

is the high-frequency vibrational mode (>1000 cm−1);
Δ ∊
is the free energy change that can be approximated by emission energy;
n
is the vibrational quantum number;
κ
is the sum of reorganization energies that involve orientational and liberational motions of the solvent and intramolecular vibration of low-frequency mode (<1000 cm−1);
k B
is the Boltzmann constant;
T
is the room temperature.

Using the state-specific approach [3], which assumes that electronic polarization is faster than nuclear polarization, we can determine the emission energy of the SGQDs and the reorganization energy of the solvent. Under this premise, this approach uses two types of solvent environments: the equilibrium solvent and the nonequilibrium solvent. It is shown that the solvent polarizations are in equilibrium with the final SGQD state based on the equilibrium solvent. On the other hand, the nonequilibrium solvent shows that the final SGQD state determines the electronic polarization of the solvent, while the initial SGQD state determines the nuclear polarization. Therefore, the emission energy was determined by calculating the energy difference between the excited-state equilibrium solvent configuration and the ground-state nonequilibrium solvent configuration [17]. The reorganization energy of the solvent is determined by calculating the energy difference between the ground-state nonequilibrium solvent configuration and the ground-state equilibrium solvent configuration [17].

The intramolecular reorganization energy is represented by:
(6)
E
intra
=
∑ l
S l
∙ ℏ
ω l
where
S l
is the Huang–Rhys factor from the low vibrational frequencies of the molecule and
ω l
is the vibrational frequency of the low-frequency mode.

The electronic coupling is defined by [3]:
(7)
H

=
Δ ∊ |
μ 01
|
(
μ 00
-
μ 11
)
2
+ 4
μ
01
2
where
μ 00
and
μ 11
are the ground state and excited state dipole moments, respectively;
μ 01
is the transition dipole moment.

Electronic conductivity is an important parameter for characterizing carrier mobility and can be calculated as follows [18]:
(8)
ϱ ∝ exp
-
E g
2
k B
T
where
E g
is the bandgap and
ϱ
is the electronic conductivity.

The B3LYP functional [19] with the 6-31g* basis set was used to optimize the ground states of all molecular systems. The photophysical properties of GQDs can be reliably described using this combination, as previous research has shown [4]. In addition, we have re-optimized the structure of the pristine GQD with the wB97XD functional [20], which incorporates Grimme's D2 dispersion model, to investigate the effects of non-bonding dispersion on the geometries of GQDs. TDDFT was used to determine the SGQD excitation energies which are broadened by a linewidth of 0.08 eV to obtain the absorption spectra. The TDDFT energy gradient was used to optimize the SGQD S1 state. Based on the frequency calculations of the optimized ground and excited states, the parameters in equation (2) were then determined using equations (3)-(6), such as the effective vibrational frequencies and Huang–Rhys factors. We determined the emission and solvent reorganization energies by applying the state-specific approach to the optimized excited states of the three molecular systems. The natural transition orbitals (NTOs) were obtained by performing unitary translation of the canonical molecular orbitals, as implemented within the Multiwfn program package [21]. Solvation was modeled using a conductor-like polarizable continuum model (CPCM) [22].

The predicted average edge CC bond length of the pristine GQD is 1.40 Å (Table 1 ), which agrees well with a previous study [3]. The long-range dispersion force does not affect the optimized structures of the GQDs used in this study, as shown by the fact that the wB97XD functional predicts the same average edge CC bond length of the SGQDs as the B3LYP functional. As shown in previous studies [23–25], the long-range dispersion force typically plays an essential role in a molecular system where significant charge redistribution occurs due to intra- or intermolecular dipole–dipole interactions. In such a situation, the long-range electrostatic interaction becomes important in describing the electronic potential surface and must be included to describe better the physical properties, such as binding energy and formation energy. However, due to the smaller physical sizes of the GQD models used in this work, which limit the delocalization of the wave functions, the influence of the long-range dispersion force on the optimized GQD structures is negligible. Nevertheless, the delocalization of the electrons increases with the size of the GQDs so that the dispersion term may gain importance in larger GQDs.

The CC bond lengths were extended to 1.41–1.42 Å by S-functionalization for both the O3HS- and SH-functionalized GQDs (Fig. 2 (a)), and the bond angles were shifted from 120.1° to 121.8° and 120.1° to 119.7°, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 (b), the CC bond length increases to 1.41–1.42 Å when C4H4S is included in the GQDs, and the bond angle decreases from 125.9° to 114.2°. This suggests that the electron density in the carbon skeleton of SGQDs was localized by the addition of S-functional groups that break the sp2 integrity of the graphene lattice. Furthermore, the Hirshfeld population analysis results showed that O3HS and C4H4S are electron-withdrawing groups, while SH is an electron-donating group (Supplemental Table S2). In particular, C4H4S is a two-site dopant as it forms two σ-bonds with adjacent carbon atoms [26], while O3HS and SH are connected to the adjacent carbon atom via one σ-bond, indicating that they are single-site dopants. The former (C4H4S) preserves the conjugated π-system, as shown by the planar geometries of GQDs (Fig. 3 (i)-(k)), while the latter (O3HS and SH) causes sp3 hybridization in the GQDs, leading to a distortion of the planar geometries of the GQDs (Fig. 3 (a)-(h)), just as in the case of GQDs functionalized with BC2O and BCO2 groups [27]. However, at a high passivation density of the C4H4S group (Fig. 3 (l)), the planarity of the GQDs is broken because the electron-attracting ability of the C4H4S group leads to an inductive effect that results in a structural distortion similar to that observed in NO2-functionalized GQDs [28]. The inductive effect of the functional group was found to be related to the extent of electron localization. The strong electron localization in the GQD carbon skeleton is a consequence of the strong electron-withdrawing or electron-donating abilities of the functional groups. NO2-functionalized GQDs, as described by Sheely et al. [28], show significant electron localization in the carbon skeleton. The planar GQD geometry can distort when the NO2 groups cover the GQD edges with a high passivation density. Moreover, part of the electron density at the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) is located at the edges of armchair GQDs [29]; therefore, differences in the bonding environments at the edges and edge coverages of SGQDs lead to a different extent of localization of the electron density in the carbon skeleton of the SGQDs.

The energy levels of the HOMO and the LUMO are essential in determining the suitability of a molecule as a light sensitizer. During photoexcitation, electrons are transported from HOMO to the LUMO of GQDs. The photoexcited electrons at the LUMO of GQDs are expected to rapidly transfer to the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2 to reduce the non-radiative recombination between photoexcited electrons and holes. This requires a higher LUMO level than the CBM of TiO2 to provide a suitable driving force for electron transfer. Moreover, the electrons at the CBM of TiO2 could recombine with the oxidized sensitizer. Therefore, a suitable redox medium (e.g., I-/I3 -) is required to regenerate the ground state of the sensitizer. Table 1 shows that the LUMO energy levels of the SGQDs are higher than the TiO2 CBM (∼–4.0 eV) [30], which means that the photoexcited electrons from the LUMO of the SGQDs can be efficiently injected into the CBM of TiO2. The highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) of SGQDs have much lower energy levels than that of I-/I3 - (–4.8 eV [31]), which means that holes can be regenerated quickly.

Compared to the pristine GQD, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels in the SH- and O3HS-functionalized GQDs shifted downward, while this happened only for the LUMO energy levels in the C4H4S-functionalized GQDs. These trends are clearly different from zigzag GQDs [32]. In the latter case, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the GQDs were shifted to higher energy levels when the GQD edges were passivated with electron-donating groups, whereas they were shifted to lower energy levels when the GQD edges were passivated with electron-withdrawing groups. This discrepancy arises from the fact that the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of the armchair and zigzag GQDs have significantly different electron density distributions. While in the former case, the FMOs are uniformly distributed over the GQD carbon skeleton, in the latter case, there is a sizable electron density at the GQD edges, which makes them extremely sensitive to the bonding environment due to the functional groups [29,33]. Therefore, the HOMO and LUMO orbitals in zigzag and armchair GQDs experience different energy shift patterns due to the attachment of electron-withdrawing and electron-donating groups to the GQD edges [28].

The bandgap describes the molecular system's chemical reactivity. The smaller the bandgap, the greater is the chemical reactivity of the molecule. Consequently, the molecular electronic conductivity increased. The bandgap of pristine GQD is 3.91 eV (Table 1), which accords well with previous reports [33,34]. The SGQD bandgaps were reduced compared to the pristine GQD, resulting in increased electronic conductivity because the localized electron density decreases the electron–electron interaction in the SGQD carbon skeleton. Consequently, the SGQD bandgaps were smaller owing to the reduced quasi-particle correction effect [32]. The SGQD passivated with six C4H4S groups has the smallest bandgap due to the significantly greater structural distortion (Fig. 3 (l)), drastically increasing electron localization in the SGQD carbon skeleton. This is similar to NO2-functionalized GQDs [28], where the full passivation of the GQD edges induces significant structural distortion, resulting in a drastic decrease in the bandgap. Moreover, the dipole moments of O3HS-functionalized GQDs were 8–10 times greater than those of SH- and C4H4S-functionalized GQDs because the O3HS group withdraws electrons and enhances the electron correlation effect in the SGQD carbon skeleton. Combined with the distorted planar geometries of the SGQDs (Fig. 3 (a)–(d)), this drastically enhances the charge redistribution and, thus, the dipole moments.

In agreement with previous reports [3,4], the calculations of this study indicate doubly degenerate HOMO and LUMO energy levels in pristine GQD (Table 2 ). S-functionalization disrupts the FMO degeneracy and is related to the SGQD structural symmetry. The HOMO and LUMO orbitals in SGQDs with symmetric structures remain degenerate or quasi-degenerate, whereas SGQDs with asymmetric structures exhibit nondegenerate HOMO and LUMO orbitals. Both the HOMO and LUMO energy levels are dominated by the electronic states of the carbon skeleton of the SGQDs bound by SH and O3HS groups (Fig. 4 (a)–(d) and (i)–(l)), whereas the HOMO energy level of the C4H4S-functionalized GQDs is determined by the hybridized states between the carbon skeleton and the C4H4S group at high edge coverage (Fig. 4 (g)–(h)). This not only affects the electronic structures of the SGQDs but also generates significant blue shifts in their absorption spectra (Fig. 5 (g)–(h)).

To further verify the accuracy of the electronic structure calculations, the optical gap of the first excited state of the pristine GQD was calculated using the standard functional (B3LYP) and the long-range corrected functional (i.e., CAM-B3LYP [35]) (Supplemental Table S3). In the absence of experimental data on the excited state properties of the pristine GQD, we compare the calculated results with those obtained by the density functional theory/multireference configuration interaction (DFT/MRCI) method [36]. The B3LYP functional predicts the optical gap more accurately and in agreement with the DFT/MRCI method, while the CAM-B3LYP functional significantly overestimates the excitation energy of the first excited state of the pristine GQD. Our conclusion is consistent with a previous theoretical study on UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs [37]. In addition, we investigate the effects of the long-range dispersion force and the larger basis set on the excited state properties of the pristine GQD by estimating the excitation energy of the first excited state using the M06 [38] and wB97X [39] functionals combined with the basis sets 6-311g and 6-311+G(d,p). Only the first excited state is investigated here since Shi et al. have shown that the prediction of the B3LYP functional at higher excited states than the first excited state agrees very well with the DFT/MRCI method [37]. Our results show that increasing the basis set from 6 to 31 g* to 6-311+G(d,p) has little effect on the excitation energy of the first excited state of the pristine GQD (Supplemental Table S3). The M06 functional slightly overestimates the excitation energy, while the wB97X functional strongly overestimates the excitation energy compared to the DFT/MRCI method. Moreover, the ZINDO/S method predicts similar excitation energy as B3LYP/6-31g* does. The ZINDO/S is a semiempirical method that successfully predicts the optical properties of polyaromatic hydrocarbons [40–42]. Therefore, the combination of B3LYP functional with 6-31g* basis set can reasonably describe the electronic and optical properties of GQD materials.

In pristine GQD, the main absorption band consists of two degenerate peaks (S3/S4 and S5/S6, respectively; see Figure S1 in the Supplemental materials). Moreover, in pristine GQD, the four least excited states (S1, S2, S3, and S4) are all dark states. For SGQDs, the brightening of these dark states is associated with the types and passivation patterns of the S-functional groups bound to the GQDs. Furthermore, the degeneracy of the S5/S6 states is broken by the asymmetric positioning of the C4H4S groups at the periphery of the SGQDs, which in turn relaxes the oscillator strengths and leads to a new absorption peak in the low energy region (Fig. 5(e) and (f)). However, a red shift in the spectra is induced only when the C4H4S groups are symmetrically arranged on the SGQD surface (Fig. 5(g) and (h)). For SGQDs associated with SH or O3HS groups, the change in symmetry leads only to a shift in the main absorption band or to a variation in absorption intensity.

As mentioned above, the ZINDO/S method can successfully describe the excited state properties of polyaromatic hydrocarbons. However, the reliability of this approach depends on the components of the system. For example, Feng et al. reported that the optical properties of GQDs containing only C, N, O, and H atoms could be well described by the ZINDO/S method, while it cannot predict the optical properties of GQDs containing N/S atoms [42]. Therefore, it is interesting to investigate the suitability of the ZINDO/S method for the current systems by comparing the absorption maxima calculated using the B3LYP/6-31g* and ZINDO/S methods. Our results show that ZINDO/S gives similar results to B3LYP/6-31g* when the edge coverages of the SGQDs are low, while the deviation becomes larger along with the edge coverages (Supplemental Table S4). Therefore, the reliability of the ZINDO/S method in predicting the optical properties of GQDs depends not only on the atomic species but also on the passivation density of the functional groups covering the edges of the GQDs.

In this investigation, it is of particular importance to study the kinetics of the nonradiative decay that begins in the lowest excited state. The non-radiative decay of high-lying excited states precedes the radiative decay according to Kasha's rule [43,44]. Therefore, the high-lying excited states first relax via a non-radiative transition to the lowest excited state, followed by electron-hole recombination. This led to the determination of the NTOs for the first excited state of SGQDs, which reveals information on electron and hole distributions that are important for nonradiative decay (Supplemental Figure S1). In pristine GQDs, the electronic transitions in the lowest excited state are typically π → π* transitions [33]. When the S-functional groups are connected to GQDs, the S-functional groups gain electron density. In particular, the C4H4S groups gained a considerable amount of electron density. In contrast, a relatively small amount of electron density was concentrated on the sulfur atoms in the other two functional groups. Therefore, the degree of electron localization in the carbon skeleton and S-functional groups is expected to affect the non-radiative decay dynamics of SGQDs attached with C4H4S groups. Finally, the SGQD oscillator strengths vanish when the S-functional groups are symmetrically bound to the GQD edges because the charge distribution leads to zero dipoles. In contrast, when the S-functional groups are asymmetrically bound to the GQD edges, the redistribution of electron density leads to nonzero transition dipole moments.

Radiative and non-radiative recombination typically depends on the separation distance between electrons and holes. The larger the distance, the more dominant a role radiative recombination plays in photoelaxation and vice versa. And the larger the distance between electrons and holes, the more localized the electronic wave function. Therefore, it is necessary to study the charge transfer character of GQDs upon photoexcitation. We calculated the charge transfer lengths (Δr) for pristine GQD and SGQDs by calculating the distance between the charge centroids of the specific molecular orbitals associated with the excitation [45]. Our results show that the Δr of the pristine GQD is orders of magnitude smaller than those of the SGQDs, suggesting that non-radiative recombination plays a dominant role in modulating photorelaxation rather than radiative recombination. This is consistent with the fact mentioned above that the first excited state in pristine GQDs is a dark state. In SGQDs, Δr decreases with the increase of the passivation density of the S-functional group, suggesting increasing the edge coverage of SGQDs increases the non-radiative recombination of electrons and holes. The calculated values of Δr are less than 2 Å, indicating that the lowest excited states in all SGQDs are not charge transfer states [45]; therefore, there is a significant Coulomb interaction between electrons and holes, accelerating non-radiative relaxation.

Since the symmetric structures of SGQDs lead to a zero transition dipole moment, the dynamics of electron-hole recombination in SGQDs with asymmetric structures was investigated (Fig. 3 (b), (f), and (g)). The calculated non-radiative relaxation times were within 0.1–10 ns, which agrees well with the reported experimental values [46,47] and theoretical results [4,48].

Electron transfer from the donor state to the acceptor state is driven by fluctuations in the photoexcited potential surface, which in turn are caused by atomic motions. For gaining insight into the phonon effect during the non-radiative transition, the Huang–Rhys factor can be used to identify the nuclear vibrational frequencies associated with this non-radiative transition. The Huang-Rhys factor describes the specific vibrational modes involved in the non-radiative relaxation process and is analogous to influential spectra under the adiabatic model [8,9].

Fig. 6 shows the calculated Huang–Rhys factor for different vibrational frequencies. According to equations (2) and (6), the intramolecular reorganization energy describes the influence of the low-frequency modes of the nucleus on the non-radiative relaxation dynamics; therefore, only the Huang–Rhys factors calculated in the high-frequency range are shown. The O3HS- and SH-functionalized GQDs were mainly coupled with defect D modes of the conjugated π-system. This suggests that electron-hole recombination in these two molecular systems is primarily determined by the extent of electron localization in the carbon skeleton. In contrast, GQD functionalized with C4H4S was coupled with a wide range of vibrational modes, suggesting that thiophene induced the additional charge relaxation channels. Moreover, the
S M
(i.e., effective Huang–Rhys factor) of the C4H4S-functionalized GQDs was smaller than those of the O3HS- and SH-functionalized GQDs because the aromaticity of thiophene reduces electron localization and thus the strength of electron–phonon coupling.

The SH-functionalized GQD showed the strongest electronic coupling among the three S-functionalized GQDs (Table 3 ) due to the smallest charge transfer length (Supplemental Figure S1), which increased the electron-hole interaction. Moreover, the electronic coupling of the GQD functionalized with the O3SH group was weaker than that of the GQD functionalized with the C4H4S group, which is consistent with the larger charge transfer length of GQD-2O3SH. Furthermore, the electronic localization and structural distortion of GQD-2O3SH also contribute to smaller electronic coupling by increasing the distance between electrons and holes.

The order of solvent reorganization energy was GQD-2O3HS > GQD-2C4H4S > GQD-2SH. The solvent reorganization energy was proportional to the square of the difference between the excited state and ground state dipole moments [17]. This indicates that sulfur oxide leads to significant charge redistribution upon photoexcitation, giving rise to the largest dipole moment difference between the ground state and the excited state. The intramolecular reorganization energies followed the order GQD-2O3HS > GQD-2SH > GQD-2C4H4S. This indicates that sulfur oxide, followed by sulfhydryl and thiophene, increased the structural flexibility of GQD.

The non-radiative decay rate is determined by the emission energy, the reorganization energy, the electronic coupling, and the effective Huang–Rhys factor in equation (2). The last exponential part in equation (2) grows as the emission energy decreases or the reorganization energy increases, increasing the non-radiative decay rate. The electronic coupling is given in the numerator of equation (2). Therefore, the non–radiative decay increases as the electronic coupling increases. According to the time-dependent Schrödinger equation, a larger non-adiabatic coupling vector leads to a faster nonradiative decay [3]. Analogous to the non-adiabatic coupling vector in the adiabatic model, the Huang-Rhys factor also plays an important role in nonradiative decay based on the diabatic model. Therefore, the non-radiative decay rate increases with an increased effective Huang-Rhys factor.

The non-radiative decay rates followed the order GQD-2SH > GQD-2C4H4S > GQD-2O3HS. This trend is consistent with electronic coupling, suggesting that electronic coupling is the most important factor mediating the non-radiative relaxation of SGQDs. GQD-2C4H4S is associated with more channels for charge relaxation and exhibits stronger electronic coupling than GQD-2O3HS; therefore, it exhibits a higher non-radiative decay rate than GQD-2O3HS.

In this work, the kinetics of non-radiative electron-hole recombination and the effect of different sulfur dopants on the optoelectronic properties of GQDs were investigated. Depending on the specific way in which the functional groups are attached to the GQDs, sulfication led to different degrees of localization of electron density in the GQD. In particular, C4H4S group provides two-site doping, which preserves the aromaticity of the carbon skeleton. Furthermore, additional absorption peaks occur in the long-wavelength range due to the asymmetric arrangement of C4H4S at the GQD edges. In contrast, for GQDs attached with the other two S functionalities, only a change in the main absorption peak or a change in absorption intensity was observed. Moreover, the considerable electron density in the GQDs was confined to the C4H4S groups, allowing more charge relaxation channels and accelerating the non-radiative decay.

Electronic coupling largely determines the overall non-radiative recombination dynamics of SGQDs. The SH-functionalized GQD exhibits the highest non-radiative decay rate because its electronic coupling is significantly stronger than the other two molecular systems. Although O3HS increases the structural flexibility of the GQD, the reduced electronic coupling combined with fewer coupled vibrational modes leads to slower non-radiative relaxation in O3HS-functionalized GQD compared to the GQD functionalized with C4H4S groups. Moreover, increasing the passivation density of the S-functional group decreases the charge transfer distance, accelerating non-radiative recombination in SGQDs.
Peng Cui: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Validation, Data curation. Yuan Xue: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122117.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
